elements.sent-2411-001: MDS codes have garnered significant attention due to their wide applications in practice.
elements.sent-2411-002: To date, most known MDS codes are equivalent to Reed-Solomon codes.
elements.sent-2411-003: The construction of non-Reed-Solomon (non-RS) type MDS codes has emerged as an intriguing and important problem in both coding theory and finite geometry.
elements.sent-2411-004: Although some constructions of non-RS type MDS codes have been presented in the literature, the parameters of these MDS codes remain subject to strict constraints.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we introduce a general framework of constructing $[n,k]$ MDS codes using the idea of selecting a suitable set of evaluation polynomials and a set of evaluation points such that all nonzero polynomials have at most $k-1$ zeros in the evaluation set.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, these MDS codes can be proved to be non-Reed-Solomon by computing their Schur squares.
elements.sent-2411-007: Furthermore, several explicit constructions of non-RS MDS codes are given by converting to combinatorial problems.
elements.sent-2411-008: As a result, new families of non-RS MDS codes with much more flexible lengths can be obtained and most of them are not covered by the known results.
elements.sent-2411-009: An $[n,k,d]$-linear code $C$ over the finite field $\F_q$ is a $k$-dimensional vector subspace of $\F_q^n$ with the minimum Hamming distance $d$.
elements.sent-2411-010: The well-known Singleton bound states that $d\le n-k+1$.
elements.sent-2411-011: If the minimum distance of a code $C$ attains the Singleton bound, then $C$ is called a maximum distance separable (MDS) code.
elements.sent-2411-012: Since the birth of coding theory, MDS codes have received great attention for their maximal error correcting capability.
elements.sent-2411-013: As a result, various properties of MDS codes have been well investigated, such as the existence of MDS codes \cite{Dau14 , classification of MDS codes \cite{Kok15 , balanced MDS codes \cite{Dau , the lowest density MDS codes \cite{Blaum and non-Reed-Solomon MDS codes \cite{RL .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In addition, MDS codes are closely connected to combinatorial designs and finite geometry \cite{Mac .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Among them, the existence of MDS codes have achieved much attention.
elements.sent-2411-016: There is a famous conjecture on MDS codes which says that the length of an MDS code over $\F_q$ is at most $q+2$, more precisely, $n\le q+1$ except for some exceptional cases \cite{Se55 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: This conjecture was proved for MDS codes over prime fields by Ball \cite{B12 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Until now, the best known MDS codes are the so-called Reed-Solomon (RS) codes and their generalizations which have found wide applications in practice.
elements.sent-2411-019: We know that there exist $[n\le q,k,n-k+1]$ Reed-Solomon (RS) codes over $\F_q$ for $1\le k\le q$.
elements.sent-2411-020: In fact, the lengths of extended generalized Reed-Solomon (GRS) codes can go up to $q+1$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, the dual of a GRS code is also a GRS code.
elements.sent-2411-022: In the literature, properties of GRS codes have been extensively studied for their wide applications such as in cryptography and distributed storage systems.
elements.sent-2411-023: In coding theory, MDS codes which are not equivalent to Reed-Solomon codes are called non-Reed-Solomon type MDS codes.
elements.sent-2411-024: Since most of the known MDS codes are equivalent to RS codes, constructing non-RS type MDS codes have become an interesting research topic.
elements.sent-2411-025: In 1989, Roth and Lempel gave a class of non-RS MDS codes via generator matrices by employing the subsets of the finite field $\F_q$ with some special properties \cite{RL .}
elements.sent-2411-026: They showed that the value of $n$ is at least $q/2+2$ for even $q$ and $3\le k\le q/2-1$, while $n\ge k+3$ for even $q$ and $q/2\le k\le q-1$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Inspired by the work \cite{Sheekey of Sheekey on twisted Gabidulin codes, Beelen {\it et al. introduced a representative work given in \cite{Beelen17, Beelen22 which proposed a new class of MDS codes called twisted RS codes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The idea of constructing twisted RS codes is using spaces of polynomials that may contain elements of degree larger than $k$ for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, twisted RS codes are not MDS in general.
elements.sent-2411-030: The authors presented two explicit subclasses of twisted RS codes that are MDS by using the properties of multiplicative subgroups and additive subgroups respectively.
elements.sent-2411-031: Thus, they can obtain MDS codes with length $(n-1)|(q-1)$ and $(n-1)|q$ that are non-RS type.
elements.sent-2411-032: However, it seems that there are strong restrictions on the lengths of these MDS twisted Reed-Solomon codes.
elements.sent-2411-033: In \cite{Beelen18 , a generalization of twisted RS codes are given by using the idea of multi-twists which includes the codes given in \cite{Beelen17 as a special case.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The corresponding dual codes are discussed as well.
elements.sent-2411-035: Following the work of Beelen {\it et al. , many work have been done on exploring the structure and properties of twisted RS codes, such as self-dual MDS codes \cite{Huang21,Sui22 , LCD MDS codes \cite{Liu21, Wu21 and near MDS codes \cite{Sui .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Recently, Chen \cite{Chen24 constructed some new non-RS MDS codes from algebraic curves.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Particularly, the author obtained some non-RS MDS codes with length $O(q^{1/4 )$ from elliptic curve codes.}
elements.sent-2411-038: It was further showed in \cite{Chen24 that the length of non-RS MDS codes can achieve $O(q^{1/k )$ from algebraic curves with higher genus under certain conditions.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Li {\it et al. designed some new cyclic MDS codes for certain parameters by determining the solutions of the system of polynomial equations \cite{LC24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: It was further shown that those codes are non-RS type.
elements.sent-2411-041: The MDS codes obtained have length $n|(q-1)$ where $q=p^r$ for certain $p$, which also has strong restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-042: Wu {\it et al. studied the extended codes of Roth-Lempel codes \cite{Wu24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: They provided a sufficient and necessary condition of such codes being non-RS MDS codes, however, they only provided explicit non-RS MDS codes over small finite fields.
elements.sent-2411-044: Therefore, the existence and explicit constructions of non-RS type MDS codes for all possible parameters remain an open problem.
elements.sent-2411-045: It is known that any MDS code of length $n$ and dimension $k$ is equivalent to a RS code for $k<3$ and $n-k<3$ \cite{Beelen17 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Thus, we only consider the construction of non-RS MDS codes for $3\le k\le n-3$.
elements.sent-2411-047: As the dual code of a RS code is again a RS code, hence we can further assume that $3\le k\le n/2$ in the following context.
elements.sent-2411-048: Motivated by the constructions in \cite{Beelen17,LC24 , we provide a general framework of constructing non-RS type MDS codes by polynomial evaluations where the polynomial space is spanned by some monomials.}
elements.sent-2411-049: More specifically, we choose pairwise distinct polynomial evaluation points $\Ga_1,\Ga_2,\dots,\Ga_n$ from $\F_q$ and some non-negative integers $i_1<i_2<\dots<i_k$ and consider the evaluation code \begin{equation}C(\bGa,I)=\{(f(\Ga_1),f(\Ga_2),\dots,f(\Ga_n)):\; f\in \span_{\F_q}\{x^{i_1},x^{i_2},\dots,x^{i_k}\}\},\end{equation} where $\bGa=\{\Ga_1,\Ga_2,\dots,\Ga_n\}$ and $I=\{i_1,i_2,\dots,i_k\}$.
elements.sent-2411-050: Without loss of generality, we may assume that $i_1=0$ (otherwise, we get an equivalent code).
elements.sent-2411-051: To show that $C(\bGa,I)$ is a non-RS MDS code, we have to show that.
elements.sent-2411-052: The length and dimension of $C(\bGa,I)$ is clear.
elements.sent-2411-053: To show the minimum distance of $C(\bGa,I)$ is $n-k+1$, it is sufficient to show that any nonzero polynomial $f(x)\in V_I:=\span_{\F_q}\{x^{i_1 ,x^{i_2 ,\dots,x^{i_k}\}$ has at most $k-1$ roots among $\Ga_1,\dots,\Ga_n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: This is equivalent to the fact that \begin{equation}\deg (\gcd(f(x),\prod_{i=1}^n(x-\alpha_i)))\le k-1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: For $k\le n/2$, to show that $C(\bGa,I)$ is not equivalent to an RS code, it is sufficient to show that the Schur square $C^{\star2 (\bGa,I)$ has dimension at least $2k$ (we refer to Subsection \ref{sub:2.3 for the definition of Schur square).}}
elements.sent-2411-056: This is because the dimension of the Schur square of an $[n,k]$ RS code with $k\le n/2$ has dimension $2k-1$ (see Subsection \ref{sub:2.3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-057: One of the main objectives of this paper is to show that $C(\bGa,I)$ are non-RS MDS codes for any non-arithmetic progression sets $I$ (we refer to Subsection \ref{sub:2.2 for the definition of arithmetic progression sets).}
elements.sent-2411-058: More precisely speaking, given $3\le k\le n/2$ and sufficiently large $q$, then for any non-arithmetic progression set $I$ of size $k$, there exists an evaluation set $\bGa$ of size $n$ such that $C(\bGa,I)$ is a non-RS MDS code.
elements.sent-2411-059: Note that $C(\bGa,I)$ is a non-RS MDS code if and only if the dual $C^\perp(\bGa,I)$ is a non-RS MDS code.
elements.sent-2411-060: Thus, we may assume that $3\le k\le n/2$.
elements.sent-2411-061: For arbitrary non-arithmetic progression set $I$, we have the following existence result for non-RS MDS codes.
elements.sent-2411-062: If $\binom{q {n >\frac{q^k-1 {q-1 \binom{m_I {k}\binom{q-k {n-k}$ with $m_I=\max\{i:\; i\in I\}$ for any non-arithmetic progression set $I\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ of size $k$, then there exists an evaluation set $\bGa$ of size $n$ such that $C(\bGa,I)$ is a non-RS MDS code.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: In particular, we may take $I=\{0,1,\dots,i-1,i+1,i+2,\dots,k\}$ with $1\le i\le k-1$, then $I$ is a non-arithmetic progression set and we have $m_I=k$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Thus, there exists an $[n,k]$-non RS MDS code as long as $3\le k\le n/2$ and $n=O\left(q^{\frac1k}\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Although our result does not improve upon \cite{Chen24 , the idea behind our construction is simpler and the required condition can be easily verified.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Another main objective of this paper is to provide explicit constructions of non-RS MDS codes for specific choices of the set $I$.
elements.sent-2411-067: In particular, we take $I=\{0,1,\dots,i-1,i+1,i+2,\dots,k\}$ with $1\le i\le k-1$.
elements.sent-2411-068: We split this case into two subcases, noting that the case where $i=k-1$ yields a more interesting result.
elements.sent-2411-069: Then there exists a $q$-ary non-RS MDS code $C(\bGa,I)$ which can be explicitly constructed, if one of the following conditions is satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-071: Let $I=\{0,1,2,\cdots,k-r-1, k-r+1,k-r+2,\cdots,k\}$ with $2\le r\le k-1$ and $3\le k\le n/2$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Compared with the results in \cite{Chen24 , the Main Theorem \ref{main:2 can largely improve that in \cite{Chen24 , especially for $r=1$ and a prime $p$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Furthermore, in comparison with the results for $(n-1)|(q-1)$ and $(n-1)|(q-1)$) in \cite{Beelen17 and $(n-1)|q$ in \cite{LC24 , the Main Theorems \ref{main:2 and \ref{main:3 offer greater flexibility in choosing code lengths, thereby allowing us to cover a larger range of lengths.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: It is obvious to see that its Schur square $C^{\star 2 (\bGa,I)$ is an $[n,2k,\ge n-2k]$ code, which has a large minimum distance.}
elements.sent-2411-075: However, the Schur square of the Roth-Lempel code \cite{RL has a significantly smaller minimum distance.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Therefore, the codes we construct are not equivalent to the Roth-Lempel codes.. \end{rem.}
elements.sent-2411-077: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-078: In section \ref{sec:2 , we introduce the definitions and properties of generalized Reed-Solomon codes, arithmetic progression sets and Schur products of linear codes.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Section \ref{sec:3 presents a general framework of non-RS MDS codes by selecting appropriate evaluation polynomials and points to ensure that all polynomials of degree at least $k$ have at most $k-1$ zeros in the set of evaluation points.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Section \ref{sec:4 provide existence results of non-RS MDS codes using combinatorial counting arguments.}
elements.sent-2411-081: In Sections \ref{sec:5.1 and \ref{sec:6 , explicit constructions of non-RS MDS codes are given by utilizing the general framework established in Section \ref{sec:3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: In this section, we review key definitions and established results related to generalized Reed-Solomon codes, arithmetic progression sets and Schur products of linear codes, which will be useful for the discussions in the following sections of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-083: Let $\F_q$ be the finite field of $q$ elements, where $q$ is a power of a prime $p$.
elements.sent-2411-084: Let $\{\Ga_1,\Ga_2,\dots,\Ga_n\}$ be the set of $n$ distinct elements of $\F_q$ and denote by $\bGa=\{\alpha_1,\Ga_2,\cdots,\alpha_n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Let $V$ be a $k$-dimensional vector subspace over $\F_q$ of the polynomial ring $\F_q[x]$.
elements.sent-2411-086: We define the evaluation map of $V$ on $\bGa$ by \begin{equation}ev_{\bGa}(\cdot): V\rightarrow \F_q^n,\quad \quad f\mapsto (f(\alpha_1),f(\alpha_2),\cdots,f(\alpha_n)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-087: If we consider $V_{k-1 =\{f(x)\in \F_q[x]: \deg(f(x))\le k-1\}$, then it is well-known that the image $ev_{\bGa (V_{k-1 )$ is the $[n,k]$ Reed-Solomon code with evaluation set $\bGa$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Since any nonzero $f\in V_{k-1}$ is of degree at most $k-1$, the polynomial $f$ has at most $k-1$ zeros.
elements.sent-2411-089: This shows that the $[n,k]$ RS code has minimum distance at least $n-k+1$, and hence it is an MDS code.
elements.sent-2411-090: Two codes $C_1$ and $C_2$ and are called equivalent if $C_2$ can be obtained from $C_1$ by a permutation of coordinates and a component-wise multiplication with some vector $\bv=(v_1,v_2,\cdots,v_n)\in \F_q^n$ of Hamming weight $n$, i.e., $v_i\neq 0$ for $i=1,2,\cdots,n$.
elements.sent-2411-091: If two codes $C_1$ and $C_2$ are equivalent, then they share the same code parameters.
elements.sent-2411-092: A generalized Reed-Solomon (GRS) code is in fact a code which is equivalent to an RS code.
elements.sent-2411-093: Choose $n$ nonzero elements $v_1,v_2,\cdots,v_n$ of $\F_q$ and put $\bv=(v_1,v_2,\cdots, v_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Then the GRS code can be defined as \begin{equation}GRS_k(\bGa,\bv):=\{(v_1f(\alpha_1),v_2f(\alpha_2),\cdots,v_nf(\alpha_n)):f(x)\in\F_q[x], \deg(f(x))\le k-1\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-095: Thus, the above GRS code is also an $[n,k,n-k+1]$ MDS code and its dual code is an MDS code as well.
elements.sent-2411-096: Otherwise, we say that a code is a non-RS MDS code if it is not equivalent to a RS code.
elements.sent-2411-097: For a finite subset $I=\{i_1,i_2,\dots,i_\ell\}\subseteq \mathbb{Z}$ of integers with $i_1<i_2<\cdots<i_\ell$, we say that $I$ is an arithmetic progression if $i_j-i_{j-1 =i_{j+1 -i_j$ for all $2\le j\le \ell-1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Otherwise, we say that $A$ is a non-arithmetic progression.
elements.sent-2411-099: Note that a set of one or two elements can be defined to be an arithmetic progression.
elements.sent-2411-100: An inverse problem in additive number theory is the problem in which we attempt to deduce properties of the set $I$ from properties of their sumset $I+I=\{a+b:\; a,b\in I\}$.
elements.sent-2411-101: The following result is the simplest case in additive number theory \cite[Theorem 1.2]{Na .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Let $I$ be a set of $\ell$ integers.. Then $|I+I|\ge 2\ell-1$.
elements.sent-2411-103: Moreover, if $I$ is a set of $\ell$ integers with $|I+I|=2\ell-1$, then $I$ is an arithmetic progression.
elements.sent-2411-104: The above Lemma \ref{lem:2.1 tells us that $I+I$ has cardinality at least $2\ell$ for any non-arithmetic progression $I$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: For any $\ell\ge 1$, there exists an arithmetic progression of $\ell$ elements.. For instance, $I=\{0,1,2,\dots,\ell-1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-106: On the other hand, for any integer $\ell\ge 3$, there also exists a non-arithmetic progression of $\ell$ elements, for example $I=\{0,1,2,3,\dots,\ell-2,\ell\}$ or $I=\{0,1,2,\dots,\ell-3,\ell-1,\ell\}$.
elements.sent-2411-107: There has been a growing interest in the Schur products of linear codes, not only as an independent object of study, but also due to its relevance in various applications of coding theory, see \cite{Cramer15, Rand15 and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-108: For two linear codes $C_1, C_2\subseteq\F_q^n$, the Schur product of $C_1$ and $C_2$ is a linear subspace of $\F_q^n$ generated by all Schur products $\bc_1\star\bc_2$ with $\bc_1\in C_1$, $\bc_2\in C_2$, namely \begin{equation}C_1\star C_2:=\span_{\F_q}\{\bc_1\star\bc_2: \bc_1\in C_1,\bc_2\in C_2\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-109: If $C_1=C_2=C$, then we call $C^{\star2 :=C\star C$ the Schur square of $C$.}
elements.sent-2411-110: If $C$ is an MDS code, then there is a lower bound given in \cite{Rand15 saying that $\dim(C^{\star2 )\ge \min\{2k-1,n\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-111: There is a criterion to determine whether an MDS code is a GRS code from \cite[Theorem 23]{MZ .}
elements.sent-2411-112: However, we need the following simpler result.
elements.sent-2411-113: Let $C\subseteq \F_q^n$ be an MDS code with $\dim(C)\le n/2$.
elements.sent-2411-114: If $\dim(C^{\star2 )\ge 2\dim(C)$, then the code $C$ is not equivalent to a Reed-Solomon code.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Let $v_1,v_2,\cdots,v_n$ be nonzero elements of $\F_q$ and $\bv=(v_1,v_2,\cdots, v_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Suppose that the code $C$ was an $[n,k]$ MDS code given by the generalized Reed-Solomon code \begin{equation}GRS_k(\bGa,\bv)=\{(v_1f(\alpha_1),v_2f(\alpha_2),\cdots,v_nf(\alpha_n)):f(x)\in\F_q[x], \deg(f(x))\le k-1\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-117: It is easy to verify that the Schur square of $C$ is \begin{equation}C^{\star 2}=GRS_{2k-1}(\bGa,\bv\star \bv).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: Hence, the dimension of Schur square $C^{\star 2}$ is $2k-1$, which is a contradiction!.
elements.sent-2411-119: For $k\le n/2$, the dimension of the Schur square of any $[n,k,n-k+1]_q$ generalized Reed-Solomon code is $2k-1$ from the proof of Lemma \ref{lem:2.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-120: Hence, any $[n,k,n-k+1]_q$ MDS code satisfying $\dim(C^{\star2 )\ge 2k$ is a non-RS MDS code.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Thus, one can determine whether it is non-Reed-Solomon or not by computing the dimension of its Schur square.
elements.section-2411-003: A general framework of non-Reed-Solomon MDS codes
elements.sent-2411-122: In this section, we provide a general framework for constructing non-Reed-Solomon (non-RS) MDS codes.
elements.sent-2411-123: A sufficient condition has been given to characterize whether a given MDS code is non-Reed-Solomon in Lemma \ref{lem:2.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-124: Reed-Solomon codes are constructed by using polynomials of degree at most $k-1$ for a code of dimension $k$.
elements.sent-2411-125: In fact, if we choose different set $V$ of evaluation polynomials, the resulting code may not be equivalent to an RS code.
elements.sent-2411-126: In this section, we focus on the construction of MDS codes which are not equivalent to RS codes by selecting $V$ to include polynomials of degree at least $ k$.
elements.sent-2411-127: However, we must carefully control the minimum distance of such codes to ensure that they are MDS codes.
elements.sent-2411-128: Now let us describe the general framework of constructing our codes as follows.
elements.sent-2411-129: Let $q$ be a prime power and $\F_q$ be the finite field with $q$ elements.
elements.sent-2411-131: Choose a vector space $V$ of dimension $k\le n/2$ in the polynomial ring $\F_q[x]$.
elements.sent-2411-132: Define the code $C$ by \begin{equation}\label{eq:1} C=\{(f(\alpha_1),f(\alpha_2),\cdots,f(\alpha_n)): f(x)\in V\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-133: Then $C$ is a non-RS MDS code if the following conditions are satisfied:.
elements.sent-2411-134: It is easy to see that the length of $C$ is $n$ and the dimension of $C$ is $k$, i.e., $C$ is an $[n,k]$-linear code.
elements.sent-2411-135: Furthermore, we can show that the code $C$ is an MDS code.
elements.sent-2411-136: It remains to prove that the minimum distance $d$ of $C$ achieves the Singleton bound $n-k+1$.
elements.sent-2411-137: Since the degree of $\gcd(f(x),\prod_{i=1 ^n(x-\alpha_i))$ is at most $k-1$ for any $f(x)\in V\setminus \{0\}$, the polynomial $f(x)$ has at most $k-1$ zeros in the set $\{\Ga_1,\Ga_2,\cdots,\Ga_n\}$, i.e., the Hamming weight of any nonzero codeword in $C$ is at least $n-k+1$.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Thus, the minimum distance of $C$ is exactly $n-k+1$ from the Singleton bound, i.e., $C$ is an $[n,k,n-k+1]$ MDS code.
elements.sent-2411-139: The main goal in the following is to choose suitable subspaces $V$ and evaluation sets $\{\Ga_1,\Ga_2,\cdots,\Ga_n\}$ such that the degree of $\gcd(f(x),\prod_{i=1 ^n(x-\alpha_i))$ is at most $k-1$ for any polynomial $f(x)\in V\setminus \{0\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-141: If there exists a subset $T$ of $F_q$ with size $n$ satisfying $T_k\cap S=\varnothing$, then the code $C(T,I)=\{(f(\alpha))_{\alpha\in T : f\in V_I\}$ is an $[n,k]$ MDS code.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Moreover, if the set $I$ is a non-arithmetic progression, then $C(T,I)$ is not equivalent to a Reed-Solomon code.. \end{prop.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Since $T_k\cap S=\varnothing$, any nonzero polynomial $f(x)$ in $V_I$ has at most $k-1$ zeros in the evaluation set $T$.
elements.sent-2411-144: It follows that the Hamming weight of codeword $(f(\alpha))_{\alpha\in T}$ is at least $n-k+1$.
elements.sent-2411-145: From the Singleton bound, the minimum distance of $C(T,I)$ is $n-k+1$.
elements.sent-2411-147: In order to show that the code $C(T,I)$ is non-Reed-Solomon, we need to determine the dimension of the Schur square of $C(T,I)$ given by $C^{\star2 (T,I)=\{(g(\alpha))_{\alpha\in T : g\in \span_{\F_q}\{x^i: i\in I+I\}\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: If $I$ is a non-arithmetic progression, then the set $I+I=\{i+j:i,j\in I\}$ has cardinality at least $2k$ by Lemma \ref{lem:2.1 .}
elements.sent-2411-149: Hence, the Schur square $C^{\star2 (T,I)$ has dimension at least $2k$.}
elements.sent-2411-150: By Lemma \ref{lem:2.2 , it is not equivalent to a Reed-Solomon code.}
elements.section-2411-004: Existence results for non-Reed-Solomon MDS codes
elements.sent-2411-151: In this section, we use a combinatorial counting argument to show that there exists at least one set $T$ such that $T_k\cap S=\varnothing$ which ensures the MDS property.
elements.sent-2411-152: Furthermore, the resulting MDS code is non-RS as long as we choose an arbitrary non-arithmetic progression.
elements.sent-2411-153: Let $S$ and $m_I$ be defined as in Section \ref{sec:3 .}
elements.sent-2411-154: Then the size of $S$ is upper bounded by \begin{equation}|S|\le \frac{q^k-1}{q-1}\binom{m_I}{k}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-155: For any monic polynomial $f\in V_I\setminus \{0\}$, $f$ has at most $m_I$ roots since $\deg(f)\le m_I$.
elements.sent-2411-156: Then the number of $k$-subsets of the zeros of $f$ in $\F_q$ is upper bounded by \begin{equation}| \{ A\subseteq\F_q: |A|=k, A\subseteq Z(f)\}|\le \binom{m_I}{k}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-157: This lemma holds true, since there are $(q^k-1)/(q-1)$ pairwise distinct monic polynomials in $V_I\setminus \{0\}$.

elements.section-2411-005: Explicit constructions of non-RS MDS codes
elements.sent-2411-182: In this section, we provide explicit constructions of non-RS MDS codes applying the framework in Section \ref{sec:3 .}
elements.sent-2411-183: In particular, we focus on the case $I=\{0,1,2,\cdots,k-2,k\}$ and $V_I=\span_{\F_q}\{x^i: i\in I\}$.
elements.sent-2411-184: In order to obtain explicit constructions of non-RS MDS codes, we need to elaborately choose the set of evaluation points $T=\{\Ga_1,\Ga_2,\cdots,\Ga_n\}$ such that any polynomial $f(x)\in V_I$ has at most $k-1$ zeros in the evaluation set $T$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Let $I$ be the set $\{0,1,2,\cdots,k-2,k\}$ and $V_I$ be the vector space spanned by the set $\{x^i: i\in I\}$ over $\F_q$.
elements.sent-2411-186: Let $T=\{\Ga_1,\Ga_2,\cdots, \Ga_n\}$ be a subset of $\F_q$ with cardinality $n$ such that all its $k$-subset sums of $T$ are nonzero, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eq:3} \sum_{j=1}^k \Ga_{i_j}\neq 0\end{equation} for all pairwise distinct elements $\Ga_{i_j}$ with $1\le i_1<i_2<\cdots<i_k\le n$.
elements.sent-2411-187: Then the code $C(T,I)=\{(f(\alpha_1),f(\alpha_2),\cdots,f(\alpha_n)): f\in V_I\}$ is an $[n,k]$ non-RS MDS code.. \end{prop.}
elements.sent-2411-188: For any nonzero polynomial $f(x)\in V_I$, the Hamming weight of the codeword $(f(\alpha_1),f(\alpha_2),\cdots,f(\alpha_n))$ is at least $n-k$.
elements.sent-2411-189: Suppose that $f(x)$ has $k$ pairwise distinct zeros $\{\Ga_{i_1 ,\Ga_{i_2 ,\cdots, \Ga_{i_k}\}$ in $T$.}}

elements.sent-2411-299: The main goal of this paper is to construct new families of non-RS MDS codes.
elements.sent-2411-300: We begin by presenting a general framework for constructing non-RS type MDS codes.
elements.sent-2411-301: This approach involves selecting an appropriate set of evaluation polynomials and a corresponding set of evaluation points such that all nonzero polynomials have at most $k-1$ zeros within the evaluation set.
elements.sent-2411-302: Additionally, both the existence and explicit constructions of these codes are provided.
elements.sent-2411-303: Constructing non-RS MDS codes for all possible lengths remains an interesting and challenging problem.
elements.sent-2411-304: However, using our framework, we believe it is possible to obtain more new families of non-RS MDS codes.
